case_name: Tebuconazole removal from well water for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for removing tebuconazole from contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town operates a groundwater well field located near agricultural land with recurrent triazole fungicide use. Monitoring has detected tebuconazole in one production well at levels that require treatment before blending into the municipal drinking water system. The utility prefers proven drinking-water processes that can be run by operators with conventional treatment experience and without high energy demand.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable tebuconazole reduction to a level consistent with drinking water protection and produce biologically safe, stable drinking water.
secondary_goals: use established treatment technology for pesticide control
maintain moderate operating complexity and cost
provide robust performance for variable low-level pesticide occurrence

core_unit_functions.pre_filtration: remove incidental particles and protect downstream adsorber performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb tebuconazole and other hydrophobic trace organics from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and polish treated water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: tebuconazole is an organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a widely established control method in drinking water treatment
raw water has low turbidity and low DOC, which supports efficient carbon adsorption and longer bed life
the utility prefers conventional and robust treatment, and GAC is compatible with moderate operator skill and moderate cost
this approach avoids the higher energy and concentrate management burden of membrane systems
potential_risks: breakthrough can occur if carbon replacement or reactivation is delayed
natural organic matter, even at modest levels, can gradually reduce adsorption capacity
performance should be verified with EBCT and pilot or design basis data for the specific pesticide mixture

core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: provide adsorptive removal of dissolved tebuconazole
core_unit_functions.rapid_mix: disperse PAC effectively into the water stream
core_unit_functions.contact_tank: allow adsorption contact time before separation

applicability_rationale: PAC is an established option for episodic or variable pesticide contamination
it can be integrated where the utility can manage chemical dosing and downstream solids removal
the contaminant is the clear primary concern and PAC offers targeted control without high energy demand
this route is attractive if contamination is intermittent and continuous GAC replacement is less desirable
potential_risks: removal depends strongly on dose, contact time, and carbon selection
solid-liquid separation must be reliable to avoid PAC carryover
continuous chemical handling and sludge generation can increase operator burden compared with fixed-bed GAC

core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize tebuconazole and transform organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration_or_gac: remove oxidation byproducts and provide additional adsorption or biodegradation polishing

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation or ozonation-based treatment is technically capable for many pesticide contaminants
post-treatment with biologically active filtration or GAC improves robustness by controlling byproducts and residual organics
this option can achieve broad micropollutant control if future contaminants become a concern
potential_risks: oxidation byproducts require evaluation and downstream polishing
ozone generation and control add operational complexity beyond the utility's preferred conventional practice
the added sophistication may be unnecessary for a single low-level pesticide problem in low-DOC groundwater

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: physically reject dissolved organic micropollutants including tebuconazole
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance after membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in finished water

applicability_rationale: pressure membrane systems are technically effective barriers for many dissolved pesticide compounds
they may be considered if broader water quality upgrading is also required beyond tebuconazole removal
potential_risks: concentrate management is a major disadvantage for a well water application
energy demand and membrane fouling control are less aligned with the stated moderate and conventional constraints
post-treatment stabilization is required and increases system complexity

core_unit_functions.uv_hydrogen_peroxide_advanced_oxidation: generate hydroxyl radicals to degrade tebuconazole and similar organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.gac_polishing: remove residual organics and oxidation transformation products

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is technically feasible for persistent organic contaminants
a polishing adsorber can improve finished-water quality after oxidation
potential_risks: high energy and reagent demand are difficult to justify for a single pesticide in otherwise good-quality well water
treatment byproduct formation and oxidant quenching need careful design and monitoring
the process is more sophisticated than needed under conventional-operation preferences
